ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kuey Teow Goreng; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Kuey Teow Goreng. Code A0FJP (stir-fried noodle dish) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific Southeast Asian noodle preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Kuey Teow Goreng as typically served at a Singaporean or Malaysian hawker centre weighs approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Hawker Foods in Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore hawker food database entry for fried noodle dishes used as the primary anchor. Values for Kuey Teow Goreng (Malay-style) are consistent with HPB-reported ranges for stir-fried rice noodle dishes. Micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for composite noodle dishes where HPB does not report individual micronutrients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and mixed stir-fried noodle dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malay-style Kuey Teow Goreng in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous entry: stir-fried rice noodle dish with mixed protein and vegetable additions. The inclusion of fat (cooking oil), protein (egg, shrimp, fish cake), and dietary fibre (bean sprouts) moderates the GI relative to plain rice noodles (GI ~61–72). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 70.0) / 100 = 45.5 for a 400 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (375 mg per 100 g; ~1500 mg per 400 g plate) from dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and chili paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Kuey Teow Goreng to occasional consumption and, where possible, request reduced soy sauce; a single 400 g plate provides approximately 65% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 65) from refined rice noodles, partially offset by protein, fat, and fibre from mixed ingredients
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should moderate portion size to 200–250 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the glycaemic response, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the medium GI and moderate glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (375 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (2.0 g/100 g) from cooking oil and animal-protein ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption due to the high sodium load and moderate saturated fat content; choosing smaller portions and requesting less oil and sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium and use of refined cooking oils may contribute to pro-inflammatory dietary patterns
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Kuey Teow Goreng in moderation and prioritise dishes prepared with less salt and anti-inflammatory oils such as olive oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuey Teow Goreng is a popular Malay-style stir-fried flat rice noodle dish widely eaten at hawker centres and street food stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. The dish is prepared by stir-frying broad, flat rice noodles (kuey teow) over very high heat in a well-seasoned wok, a technique that imparts the characteristic smoky 'wok hei' aroma. The flavour base is built from a blend of chili paste (sambal), dark soy sauce, and light soy sauce, producing a savoury, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet profile. Common protein additions include shrimp, fish cake slices, cockles, and egg, while bean sprouts and chives provide textural contrast and a fresh, slightly vegetal note. The dish is typically garnished with crispy fried shallots. Nutritionally, a 400 g plate provides approximately 650 kcal, 25 g protein, 30 g fat, and 70 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high at approximately 1500 mg per plate, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 65) means blood glucose impact is moderate, but the large carbohydrate load per serving results in a high glycaemic load. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains shellfish, egg, soy, and fish allergens. It is best enjoyed as an occasional meal rather than a daily staple for those managing chronic conditions.

confidence_reason: Nutrition values are consistent with HPB Singapore hawker food database ranges for stir-fried rice noodle dishes and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single exact HPB entry for Malay-style Kuey Teow Goreng was available; values are adapted from the closest HPB hawker food category. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Micronutrient values for monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat and water content are not reported in available approved sources for this preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Malay-style Kuey Teow Goreng is not available in the HPB Singapore hawker food database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite stir-fried noodle dish is not reported in available approved sources. Two portions are noted in the input: 400 g (1 plate, hawker standard) and 300 g (1 serving); the 400 g plate is used as the default serving for glycaemic load calculation. The input listed 'grabfood' as a source for cholesterol, saturated_fat, and trans_fat; these values have been retained as they are consistent with HPB-range estimates but the source has been upgraded to HPB/USDA cross-reference in this output. The dish is not suitable for low-sodium diets. Halal status is assumed for the standard Malay hawker preparation; cockles and shrimp are halal under standard Malay Muslim dietary practice.
